title: Apply: First Amendment: Five Freedoms
description: Apply your understanding of the five freedoms covered in the First Amendment with this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the concurring opinion in *Whitney v. California* (1927), written by Justice Brandeis.**

Fear of serious injury cannot alone justify suppression of free speech and assembly. Men feared witches and burnt women. It is the function of speech to free men from the bondage of irrational fears. To justify suppression of free speech, there must be reasonable ground to fear that serious evil will result if free speech is practiced. There must be reasonable ground to believe that the danger [[☃ definition 1]] is [[☃ definition 2]]. There must be reasonable ground to believe that the evil to be prevented is a serious one.

**Which statement best summarizes Justice Brandeis’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The government can limit a person’s freedom of speech only if an immediate danger can result from that speech.
The right to speak freely cannot have any limits in a free democracy, as freedom of conscience is a natural right.
The government can limit freedom of speech only if the limitation applies to all ideas, regardless of content.
The right to express one’s religious beliefs in public cannot be limited by the government, even if the speech affects public safety.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the concurring opinion in *Whitney v. California* (1927), written by Justice Brandeis.**

Fear of serious injury cannot alone justify suppression of free speech and assembly. Men feared witches and burnt women. It is the function of speech to free men from the bondage of irrational fears. To justify suppression of free speech, there must be reasonable ground to fear that serious evil will result if free speech is practiced. There must be reasonable ground to believe that the danger [[☃ definition 1]] is [[☃ definition 2]]. There must be reasonable ground to believe that the evil to be prevented is a serious one.

**Using Justice Brandeis’s reasoning in the excerpt, which of these scenarios would be a reasonable limitation on freedom of speech?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A city council prohibits the use of loudspeakers after 10 p.m.
A police officer arrests a woman after she drives to her brother’s house and threatens to hurt him.
A campus police officer forces a student group protest to disperse for fear that it might eventually lead to unrest.
A public university president cancels a professor’s lecture because it discusses radical social change, including illegal actions taken by radicals.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the [[☃ definition 1]] in *Brandenburg v. Ohio* (1969).**

The Court upheld the statute on the ground that, without more, “advocating” violent [[☃ definition 2]] to effect political and economic change involves such danger to the security of the State that the State may outlaw it. . . . But [an earlier decision] has been thoroughly discredited by later decisions. . . . These later decisions have fashioned the principle that the constitutional guarantees of free speech and free press do not permit a State to forbid or [[☃ definition 3]] advocacy of the use of force or of law violation except where such advocacy is directed to inciting or producing [[☃ definition 4]] lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action. . . .

Measured by this test, Ohio’s Criminal Syndicalism Act cannot be sustained.

**Using the Court’s reasoning in the excerpt, which scenario would be an example of an unreasonable limitation on free speech?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an opinion issued "by the court" without naming an individual author
a method or way of doing something

choices.content: A woman is fined for playing loud music in her yard and  violating a noise level ordinance in her county.
A man is fined for publishing an article that includes false statements about his neighbor and damaging his neighbor’s reputation.
A police officer arrests a man dining with friends at an outdoor cafe and discussing the best way to commit a robbery.
A police officer arrests a woman for preventing a construction group from tearing down a tree by tying herself to it.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Pennsylvania Declaration of Rights (1776).**

That all men have a natural and unalienable right to worship Almighty God according to the [[☃ definition 1]] of their own consciences and understanding: And that no man ought or of right can be compelled to attend any religious worship, or erect or support any place of worship, or maintain any [[☃ definition 2]], contrary to, or against, his own free will and consent.

**This excerpt best reflects which fact about early America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the work of a particular religion or religious person

choices.content: Some state constitutions protected freedom of speech during the American Revolution.
Some colonies established churches that were supported by public funds and that required attendance.
Some colonies limited the practice of non-Christian religions because the king commanded that they do so.
Some state constitutions recognized the right to religious liberty before the ratification of the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Pennsylvania Declaration of Rights (1776).**

That all men have a natural and unalienable right to worship Almighty God according to the [[☃ definition 1]] of their own consciences and understanding . . . 

**The protections described in the excerpt are similar to those in which part of the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Free Exercise Clause
The Establishment Clause
The Free Speech Clause
The Free Press Clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1789 letter from Thomas Jefferson to James Madison.**

I must now say a word on the declaration of rights you have been so good as to send me. I like it as far as it goes; but I should have been for going further. For instance the following alterations and additions would have pleased me: Art. 4. “The people shall not be deprived or abridged of their right to speak, to write or otherwise to publish any thing but false facts affecting injuriously the life, liberty, property, or reputation of others or affecting the peace of the [[☃ definition 1]] with foreign nations.”

**Which statement best summarizes the addition Jefferson asks Madison to include in the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The government cannot stop people from reaching out to their representatives to tell them about problems in their communities.
The government cannot prevent people from printing news unless the information defames someone or threatens national security.
The government cannot force people to think specific thoughts or worship particular religions.
The government cannot stop people from joining groups that hold similar beliefs to their own.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1789 letter from Thomas Jefferson to James Madison.**

I must now say a word on the declaration of rights you have been so good as to send me. I like it as far as it goes; but I should have been for going further. For instance the following alterations and additions would have pleased me: Art. 4. “The people shall not be deprived or abridged of their right to speak, to write or otherwise to publish any thing but false facts affecting injuriously the life, liberty, property, or reputation of others or affecting the peace of the [[☃ definition 1]] with foreign nations.”

**The concerns Jefferson outlines are reflected in which part of the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Free Exercise Clause
The Assembly Clause
The Free Speech Clause
The Petition Clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the [[☃ definition 1]] in *Brandenburg v. Ohio* (1969). In the lower course decision that the Court is reviewing, Clarence Brandenburg was convicted under Ohio law for advocating violence at a Ku Klux Klan rally.**

The Court [previously] upheld the statute on the ground that, without more, “advocating” violent [[☃ definition 2]] to effect political and economic change involves such danger to the security of the State that the State may outlaw it. . . . But [this earlier decision] has been thoroughly discredited by later decisions. . . . These later decisions have fashioned the principle that the constitutional guarantees of free speech and free press do not permit a State to forbid or [[☃ definition 3]] advocacy of the use of force or of law violation except where such advocacy is directed to inciting or producing [[☃ definition 4]] lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action. . . .

Measured by this test, Ohio’s Criminal Syndicalism Act cannot be sustained.

**Which statement best describes the Supreme Court's ruling in this case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an opinion issued "by the court" without naming an individual author
a method or way of doing something

choices.content: The Court upheld the Ohio law, arguing that advocating violence, even without directly calling for imminent lawless action, is not protected speech.
The Court broadened the circumstances under which speech could be restricted, particularly in cases of hate speech.
The Court ruled that all speech, including advocacy of violence, is protected under the First Amendment.
The Court struck down the Ohio law, protecting speech unless it is both intended to and likely to cause immediate illegal activity.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 article published in Philadelphia's *Freeman’s Journal* under the [[☃ definition 1]] Centinel II.**

Friends, Countrymen, and Fellow-citizens, As long as the liberty of the press continues unviolated, and the people have the right of expressing and publishing their [[☃ definition 2]] upon every public measure, it is next to impossible to enslave a free nation. . . . 

Cannot Congress, when possessed of the immense authority proposed to be [[☃ definition 3]], restrain the printers, and put them under regulation. — Recollect that the [[☃ definition 4]] of the federal legislature over the State establishments is recognized by a special article, [[☃ definition 5]] — “that this Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof, and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the *supreme law* of the land; and the judges in every State shall be bound thereby, any thing in the *Constitutions* or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding.” — After such a declaration, what security does the *Constitutions* of the several States afford for the *liberty of the press and other invaluable personal rights,* not provided for by the new plan? — Does this sweeping clause subject every thing to the control of Congress?

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Freedom of the press is necessary only when a government is tyrannical and seeks to oppress its people.
Any federal law that violates states’ constitutions will be considered unconstitutional under the Supremacy Clause.
The Tenth Amendment ensures that states have the power to protect freedom of the press, regardless of what the federal government does.
The  powers given to Congress under the Supremacy Clause can undermine the freedom of the press guaranteed by state constitutions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 letter published in the *New York Journal* under the [[☃ definition 1]] Cincinnatus.**

I have proved, sir, that not only some power is given in the constitution to restrain, and even to [[☃ definition 2]] the press, but that it is a power totally unlimited; and may certainly [[☃ definition 3]] the freedom of the press, and convert it from being the [[☃ definition 4]] of liberty to become an engine of imposition and tyranny. It is an easy step from restraining the press to making it place the worst actions of government in so favorable a light, that we may groan under tyranny and oppression without knowing from whence it comes. . . . 

[S]uch men as Milton, Sidney, Locke, Montesquieu, and Trenchard, have thought it essential to the preservation of liberty against the artful and [[☃ definition 5]] [[☃ definition 6]] of those with whom power is trusted. You will pardon me, sir, if I pay some respect to these opinions, and wish that the freedom of the press may be previously secured as a constitutional and unalienable right, and not left to the [[☃ definition 7]] care of popular privileges which may or may not influence our new rulers.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: cause to be under the authority of someone or something
acts of taking more and more of some thing or right that doesn’t belong to you

choices.content: The Constitution needs to secure citizens’ freedom of press, as the federal government’s power under the Constitution poses a potential threat.
The Constitution does not specifically list what powers the government has over the press, making it unclear how the federal government could restrict the press.
The Constitution needs to regulate the powers of the press, as unfair or misleading press coverage can undermine the work of the government.
The Constitution does not need to explicitly protect the freedom of the press, as this freedom is understood to be a natural right given to all people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 article published in Philadelphia's *Freeman’s Journal* under the [[☃ definition 1]] Centinel II.**

For universal experience demonstrates the necessity of the most [[☃ definition 2]]declarations and restrictions, to protect the rights and liberties of mankind, from the silent, powerful and ever active conspiracy of those who govern. . . .

[T]here is no declaration, that all men have a natural and unalienable right to worship Almighty God, according to the [[☃ definition 3]] of their own consciences and understanding; and that no man ought, or of right can be [[☃ definition 4]] to attend any religious worship, or erect or support any place of worship, or maintain any [[☃ definition 5]], contrary to, or against his own free will and consent; and that no authority can or ought to be vested in, or assumed by any power whatever, that shall in any case interfere with, or in any manner control, the right of conscience in the free exercise of religious worship . . . all which is omitted to be done in the proposed government. . . . 

**The author would most likely agree with which statement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the work of a particular religious group

choices.content: The government’s powers are listed in the Constitution, ensuring that the government cannot interfere with people’s religious freedom.
The Constitution doesn’t do enough to safeguard individual liberties and needs amendments to protect the people’s religious freedom.
Each state is responsible for protecting its citizens’ religious freedoms, as the Constitution does not explicitly protect those rights.
The Constitution should not list specific rights like freedom of religion, as any rights not listed will not be protected.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Alexander’s 1737 letter to the editor of the *Philadelphia Gazette*.**

The abuses of freedom of speech are the [[☃ definition 1]] of Liberty. They ought to be [[☃ definition 2]]; but whom dare we commit the care of doing it? An evil [[☃ definition 3]],  entrusted with power to *punish Words,* is armed with a Weapon the most *destructive* and *terrible*. Under the [[☃ definition 4]] of [[☃ definition 5]] the [[☃ definition 6]] branches, he frequently destroys the tree.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an official who administers the law

choices.content: There need to be more limits on free speech; otherwise, citizens can use their words to violate the freedoms of those in minority groups.
Any limit on freedom of speech imposed by the government destroys liberty and endangers the natural rights with which people are born.
Although there should be limits to freedom of speech, giving the government too much authority to restrict speech can be dangerous.
While freedom of speech is a civil liberty every person should have, no form of speech is free from consequences or government penalties.
